ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for peach gum dessert; mapped to the nearest parent class 'dessert food product' (FOODON_03315091). A more specific term for Chinese sweet soup desserts is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Sweet soups and puddings) is the closest defensible category for this Chinese sweet soup dessert. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for peach gum tong sui.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for peach gum dessert or Chinese sweet soup dessert category as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of peach gum dessert is one bowl (approximately 250 g), consumed warm or chilled as a dessert or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Peach Gum Dessert — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Peach Gum Dessert (cr_id F02050012), confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken as-is from the HPB record. Saturated fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g consistent with 0.0 g total fat; this is confirmed zero by formulation (the dessert contains no fat-bearing ingredients in material quantity). Micronutrient data not reported by HPB; set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on sweetened Chinese dessert soup with sugar and dietary fibre
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peach gum dessert in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous Chinese sweet soup desserts with comparable sugar content (~8–9 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (~2 g/100 g) that moderate glycaemic response. Rock sugar (sucrose-based) has a GI of approximately 65; the presence of 2.11 g/100 g dietary fibre from peach gum and white fungus attenuates the glycaemic response, supporting a low GI estimate (GI 55 is considered low). Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 9.27 × 2.5) / 100 = 12.75, rounded to 12.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g); moderate added sugar with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Peach gum dessert is very low in sodium (5 mg/100 g) and does not trigger any hypertension hard gate; however, individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sugar preparation and limit portion size to one bowl, as the 8.69 g/100 g added sugar with negligible potassium provides no cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar 8.69 g/100 g with low GI (55); dietary fibre 2.11 g/100 g provides partial glycaemic attenuation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should treat peach gum dessert as an occasional indulgence only, limiting intake to a small portion (100–150 g) and requesting preparation with minimal or no added rock sugar, as the 8.69 g/100 g sugar load with a low estimated GI of 55 will produce a meaningful postprandial glucose rise despite the partial buffering from 2.11 g/100 g dietary fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and sodium; low calorie density; moderate added sugar is the primary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peach gum dessert is very low in fat (0 g/100 g), saturated fat (0 g/100 g), and sodium (5 mg/100 g), making it a low cardiovascular-risk dessert option; individuals with cardiovascular disease should nonetheless limit portion size and request reduced-sugar preparation to minimise the contribution of added sugar to overall cardiometabolic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar 8.69 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no confirmed anti-inflammatory ingredient at material quantity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prepare peach gum dessert without added sugar and include snow fungus and red dates to improve the anti-inflammatory profile, as the standard preparation's 8.69 g/100 g added sugar promotes systemic inflammation without any confirmed offsetting anti-inflammatory factor at material quantity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peach gum dessert (桃胶甜汤) is a traditional Chinese sweet soup widely consumed in Singapore and Southern China as a dessert or snack. It is prepared by soaking dried peach gum (the resinous sap secreted by peach trees, Prunus persica) overnight until it swells into a translucent, jelly-like mass, then simmering it with white fungus (snow fungus, Tremella fuciformis), dried longan, and rock sugar in water for 30–60 minutes. The resulting dessert has a lightly sweet, floral flavour with a soft, gelatinous texture from the peach gum and a delicate crunch from the white fungus. It is served warm or chilled. Nutritionally, peach gum dessert is low in calories (38 kcal/100 g), contains negligible fat (0 g/100 g), and provides moderate carbohydrate (9.27 g/100 g) predominantly as sugar (8.69 g/100 g from added rock sugar). Dietary fibre is 2.11 g/100 g, contributed by the peach gum polysaccharides and white fungus beta-glucans. Sodium is very low at 5 mg/100 g. The dessert is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is gluten-free. Clinically, the moderate added sugar content (8.69 g/100 g) and estimated low glycaemic index (55) make it an occasional treat for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease; preparation with reduced or no rock sugar significantly improves its suitability for these groups. The peach gum itself is traditionally valued in Chinese wellness culture for its collagen-like properties and skin-nourishing reputation, though these claims are not yet fully substantiated by clinical evidence.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02050012, confidence 0.95) with exact match. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) based on comparable Chinese sweet soup desserts from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB source match for all macronutrients.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02050012); this dessert contains no fat-bearing ingredients in material quantity — confirmed zero by source and formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with 0.0 g total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this entry and not available in any approved secondary source for this specific preparation as of 2026. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match for this specific Chinese sweet soup preparation as of 2026. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for peach gum dessert is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 23.175) / 100 = 12.75, rounded to 12.8.
